sentences.text: Nobody, just an SS-- a, a man who was a driver.
Yes.
Malnutrition.
And too low blood pressure.

After delousing, and I had to stay a night in the hospital, I was allowed to-- anyone who had family there-- some who didn't have family anymore, which were taken out by the so-called [NON-ENGLISH], were taken over by the groups.
Group, the [INAUDIBLE].
Two weeks.
Back to Salaspils.
But in the ghetto, nobody knew what was going on in Salaspils.
And I had to tell more lies to friends and relatives than I have told in my whole life.
Because I had buried their husbands.
I know they were shot and things like that.
Like I never told my mother-in-law or my wife till a few years ago, that her husband or her father got shot in Germany in 1942.
They did not know that.
We came together and said by not telling the truth, we would be better off.
We would create a panic in the ghetto.
We were afraid.
Because there were 1,000 people dead.
We, we made that decision in the hospital.
Because we didn't know anything till we were in the hospital, found out when they ask questions.
Well, it was May 1st.
They gave more food in Salaspils, but still not enough to keep you alive, but just slow the process of starvation down a little bit.
And then they went and gave people the thought for the next two weeks, you are on vacation.
You don't have to go out.
And you will get double rations if you are in the malnutrition.
But then the worst part is, so for the camp administration, which were well nourished-- or camp police and things like that-- they sent them for RR to the ghetto.
And there was an uproar in the ghetto over that.
Yeah.
There was an uproar in the ghetto when they come back, that the healthy ones were sent to the ghetto for rest and recreation and the sick ones were off to Salaspils.
But the ghetto must have known what was going on in Salaspils, because there were not always 1,500 Jews there, on the ration.
The Germans gave-- I don't think that the Germans gave over the rations for 1,500 Jews when they-- so many were dead every day.
But they're pretty dumb in the ghetto.
I was assigned in a hospital-- in Sala-- in a barrack hospital, even though I had no knowledge of it.
And well, let's say above the naval, aspirin, below the naval, iodine.
That was it.
I had to go over the work kommando, on the second shift then, afterwards, choose either [INAUDIBLE] and a sawmill.
Somebody got injured to put a dressing on.
Or somebody had temperature.
But if the temperature was under 102, don't give him any aspirin, because there is not enough aspirin around.
And some days I worked on a [NON-ENGLISH], on a sawmill and some days stayed in a hospital.
And then the Latvian SS, what search us when we arrive-- and if they found somebody-- something on somebody what you feared-- you could trade for food, they were beating up.
They would hit him so much that he was bleeding all over.
Although I had a job, sometimes I have a dozen fellows to bandage, half the night, to stop the bleeding and everything.
Till probably middle of July, '42.
But one day, I was working in, in a-- in the hospital.
And I was disinfecting some instruments.
And the only way I could disinfect them was burning them.
But there was all the rule-- you couldn't make any fire on your camp in summertime-- not at all.
So I made some fire in the oven in the hospital.
And some smoke must have come out of the smoke stack.
The first thing what I know, I got a slap in the face on the part where the instruments were flying on the floor.
When [?
Nichol, ?] the commandant-- when Einstein, the [INAUDIBLE], saw what they had done, they-- I said, pick up the instrument.
And I got instructed by [?
Nichol ?] that I could burn the instruments.
But every time I make a fire, I have to notify Einstein or the camp administration.
Because it is against the rule.
Because every time Lange came in the camp and he saw fire, and saw Jews heating up-- heating up on a fire, he would open up fire and kill one or two.
So I saw him shooting a number of people, just at random, or when-- in January '42 a transport-- four boxcars came by the camp.
We had to unload.
First one was full of suitcases-- the first two, boxcars.
The second was-- were filled with knapsack and outer clothing with the Stars of David on it.
And we start plundering food.
Lange came out with Maiwald.
And the next thing I heard was three shots.
When I looked out at the boxcar, I see that one's lying on the floor.
One was Hans [INAUDIBLE].
He was from [INAUDIBLE] that was a transport Dusseldorf.
And then they put Latvian SS to chase us even more that we should do the work faster and instruct the Latvian SS, if you see a Jew picking anything up like he's not supposed to pick up, shoot him.
Middle of July, '42 the Germans start taking out the Jews back to the ghetto.
Because they were putting gentile Latvians in.
First they had put in women.
Now they were putting males in and putting a big fence around the camp, also.
And I was marched back to the ghetto.
And I was 24 hours in the ghetto.
And then I had to stand up for roll call.
And I was selected to work in a motor pool for the Gestapo-- or [GERMAN], what they called.
That was the killer kommando who did the mass killings.
I was on [GERMAN].
The Germans called it [GERMAN], in Riga.
For 10 months.
Yes.
When we marched in, we got greeted by two SS NCOs, [GERMAN], Walter [?
Weiderman ?] and Heinrich-- no, yeah.
Wait a minute, Walter [?
Weiderman-- ?] no, I can't remember the name of the other.
He-- Lad-- Heinrich [?
Ladovitch. ?] [?
Ladovitch ?] greeted us with a rubber truncheon.
Weiderman ?] was nearly seven feet tall.
He only had to kick or to slap somebody.
And then we were assigned to work.
And I'd always had to go fast.
And if you made a mistake, you had a kick in the rear or you had a slap.
And you would not get any food.
And you had to work 10 hours a day.
I was assigned to an [GERMAN].
That's an NCO, staff sergeant-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dausig.
He was from Osnabruck.
And every time I was on the road with him, I was treated like a human being.
He even shared with me what he had to eat.
From--
Traveling with him.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he also could be a bastard to other Jews.
But every time I was out with him, he treated me fair.
But the only thing was, in any weather, I always had to sit at-- in the back of the truck, in an open truck.
We were allowed to sit in the front with him.
During this time, I lived with the family.
But only one thing I was lucky was sleep.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, I was in the ghetto of-- earlier than 6 o'clock in the evening.
My mother was making food.
And I was on the ration what we had.
But I had-- let's say we are making a joke.
The cow's asleep but it's chained to the floor so the bedbugs won't carry it away.
You see, my legs-- everything was scratched up from the bedbugs, you know, during the night.
You were a lack of sleep.
And sometimes you slept through it.
You were too tired, you know.
And it was terrible.
We heard some rumors.
But were-- a few things were unusual.
Came Yom Kippur in 1942.
We were marched in the main building of the-- where the SS were.
There was a field kitchen.
And we got food.
It was Yom Kippur-- they fed us.
And from that day on, we got every day a quart of soup.
Because they found out Jews who can't eat-- don't eat and are undernourished cannot do the work.
Then, October-- I mean, there were some killings.
We had to take some body-- some bodies away.
Some were thrown out on the third floor, from the window-- Jews and Latvians alike, from the interrogation.
We had to take them from the prison cells, the bodies.
Some were killed right in the motor pool, where the cars were washed and everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, middle of October 1942, Dausig came on and said, get some planks and heavy boards on a-- on a truck and pick out 10 other Jews.
To me.
And I looked at him.
Yeah.
And then you go in the kitchen with two and get 10 loaves of bread.
We are going on a trip.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then he said, tell your group leader you will not be coming-- going home tonight.
You go on a trip with me.
You be coming back in the ghetto by tomorrow.
That's all I knew.
And we went on a trip.
We went to Kaunus, Kovno, Lithuania.
And he wanted to put us in a ghetto there, but you couldn't.
So we stayed somewhere on the road.
And then he made some phone calls.
Then we went to a place.
And here we have a couple of buses and a smashed car and some Latvian SS.
Well, the Latvian SS was drunk.
So we looked in the buses.
And they were full of clothing with Stars of David.
And here, the Latvian SS told us they're coming from Minsk.
And they just liquidated the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we wanted something to eat.
And the SS, Dausig, said, well, take some clothing, take off the Stars of David, and trade it in for food.
And get me a couple of sides of bacon.
My mother in Osnabruck doesn't have enough to eat either.
So we got to-- we traded with a farmer for food.
And we cooked some food.
And we had plenty to eat-- more than we could eat.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we had change the tires on one bus.
And the other one we had to tie-- tie to the truck-- had to be towed in.
And the car we had to load on a truck, because the whole front end was smashed, because he had run into the bus when he was drunk.
And we looked through.
And we saw Stars of David without inscription on it.
And we saw Stars of David, which originated in Germany on the clothing.
Mostly were woolen clothing-- sweaters and things like that.
Jews had been shot.
And they had told us that they liquidated the ghetto in Minsk.
So they were bragging about it.
And--
We went-- we went-- next day, we went back to Riga.
And we asked Dausig if you could drop us off on the ghetto, those who were from the ghetto.
Of course, we would like to take the food in.
We could not be able-- would not be able to take it in.
And he asked, what do you mean you cannot take in?
So we told him our Tuchel and Nallmann-- they're the two police officers who shake us when we come in the ghetto.
And if we have illegal food, we will be executed.
Now Tuchel and Nallmann were two NCOs from the police.
And they checked every day Jews coming back in the ghetto from the work kommandos.
And when they were drunk, they were molesting the women.
If a woman had a little bit up front in the bosoms, one would say to the other one, I wonder what she's hiding up there, and would put a hand in her blouse, or in the-- and some under the skirts.
Quite often.
I also saw that they found some food on-- illegal food, they would beat him up and turn him over to the ghetto commandant.
And then they would be in prison.
And on the weekends, they would be shot.
And it always came up.
And the bastards was the most dangerous when they were drunk.
They were in the middle of their 20s.
And I testified against them in 1970, in Hamburg, on the trial.
Tuchel got life.
And Nallmann got 50 years.
But they already had spent close to 20 years in a Russian prison.
Yeah.
From motor pool of-- in Salaspils, in the prison, in the hospital.
Yes, all [INAUDIBLE].
Not in Salaspils.
In the ghetto, you hear something, because people saw newspapers and things but-- all-German newspaper.
And some heard something illegal on a radio-- so things like that.
And not much was going on till 1943.
Then we heard more.
When I worked in the motor pool for the Gestapo, one day we marched by the railroad station.
They had a newspaper where it was black lines around.
And it says, Stalingrad has fallen.
That's the German army in Stalingrad had-- had to surrender.
Then we knew that-- it's the first time, really, that something was going on.
Now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to cut in and I have to tell you something, which may surprise you.
My family has the most survivors from any family who were in the ghetto thanks to my sister-in-law and her aunt.
Her aunt was the secretary from [?
Shols, ?] the chief of the labor office.
For the Riga ghetto.
And she made sure that we had good work kommandos, where something was to eat and also that nobody would get [NON-ENGLISH].
That means housed outside of the ghetto.
The family stayed together.
And that is how we survived.
My oldest brother, Kurt, who died in Salaspils.
Hilda.
And, and her maiden name was Zander-- Z-A-N-D-E-R.
And her aunt was Emma Harf, H-A-R-F.
And her husband was a policeman in the ghetto.
She made sure that we were [INAUDIBLE].
And my sister worked in a clothing depot in the ghetto.
And my brother-in-law was a policeman.
OK, that is where the Latvian Jews were.
They were separated from this German ghetto till October, 1942.
Then a couple of Latvian Jews had escaped.
And the Germans found out.
And then they surrounded the ghetto.
I was all day in the ghetto.
I had burns from caustic soda on my arms and I had to go to the hospital.
And on my way to the hospital, I got stopped by the police and pushed in a house.
On here, near the [NON-ENGLISH].
And here, I saw that they were selecting Latvian Jews and putting them on the tracks in the Latvian ghetto.
And then they marched the police, 30 policemen, on the [NON-ENGLISH].
And then they had to line up in one row.
When they were lined up in a row, Lange took the machine pistol off his shoulder, and some others too.
One was Zoger.
The other I can't remember.
There were quite a few.
And one of the policemen yelled, run!
So they started to run.
And the Germans opened fire.
And they got shot.
But, but when the Germans counted, to find out one had escaped.
So they searched through the houses and they found this one and two other Jews in a basement.
They marched them on the [NON-ENGLISH] and [?
Krauzer ?] shot all three.
And one SS man also got killed in the firing.
I watched that.
Yeah.
And there was even a third ghetto, a small part, where close to 300 women were-- Latvian women.
Yes.
Hope that the Germans would lose the war.
But at the same time, we were under no illusion.
We said, they may kill us all before.
There were-- we had no change in the rations.
There was not enough.
And you had to get illegal food to-- to sustain.
Now-- most of the army kommandos where the Jews were were giving soup to the Jews.
When I found out, from my sister-in-law, that the Jews who worked in the motor pool for the Gestapo-- we are supposed to be separated from the ghetto.
And she told me, get out of that work kommando.
I took my bread ration to a Dr.
Frankenbash, ?] who was a [? group ?] doctor from Dusseldorf, I gave him my ration and I asked him to give me a slip that I had a hernia.
Because you had to be healthy to work in a motor pool.
Of course, that was very heavy work.
And it worked.
And I was assigned to the army closing depot for two weeks, which was the worst work kommando in the ghetto, but I could care less.
I was assigned to the army bakery.
No, wait a minute, no-- I became group leader of a work kommando-- [NON-ENGLISH].
That was the army was collecting scrap metal for recycling in Germany.
And the Germans were dismantling the biggest cotton mill in Latvia.
That was May.
Yes.
And there were-- in charge was a sergeant.
He said, you do your work and don't bother me as long you move.
But [INAUDIBLE], who was in charge of Lange, an SS lieutenant, wanted to have it done faster.
And I told him, well, we can do it faster.
But there's only one condition.
You have to give us some food.
Because on an empty stomach, they cannot work.
And we got-- let's talk to the people-- let's talk to the chief in the kitchen.
And we got food.
In the morning, we got food when we arrived.
In the [INAUDIBLE] we got soup.
So it was a good work kommando.
And we could even take some wood or some soup home for our families and wood for cooking.
I stayed six weeks then I was in what finished.
Then I got assigned to see army bakery, which was one of the best work kommandos.
Because the army would feed you.
And you could take bread home.
And if you didn't feel-- you didn't have enough, you would drop some bread.
You would get a kick in the rear.
But anything on the floor was for the Jews anyway.
But the work was heavy.
You worked all day like [INAUDIBLE]-- had to carry backs this flour-- 100 kilograms, was about 220 pounds.
Yes.
And I was there till, oh, must have been end of August, probably.
We marching out of the ghetto.
Then [INAUDIBLE], the commandant for Jungfernhof, pulled me out and said, you're going with me to Jungfernhof for the harvest.
So I had no other choice.
End of August.
Yeah.
In his car.
Car-- five.
Yeah.
Probably four weeks.
And then I went back to the ghetto.
But then it wasn't-- ghetto was-- was already being dissolved.
Systematic, every few days a transport would go to concentration camp Kaiswerwald.
And two weeks later, I was in Kaiserwald.
Yes.
They were going on already before.
And we knew that Kaiserwald was no sanatorium.
We knew that already.
On trucks.
30 or 40 on a truck each time.
You-- they were taking 200 at a time, each time.
Yes.
I had no other choice.
My parents and my younger brother was-- and sister-in-law and my sister and brother-in-law were still in the ghetto.
Big, barbed wire fence, guard posts, and barracks.
Wooden barracks.
Miserable.
I would say yes.
Because we were greeted by antisemitic Poles and German criminals.
There were maybe 1,000 Jews in Kaiserwald, in two barracks, and maybe 1,000 Poles and a few hundred German criminals.
And the SS who was in charge came from the concentration camp Oranienburg.
The Jews-- equal to-- there was no problem.
But the, the Poles were very abusive.
And the Germans were even worse.
First, we got stripped of everything what we had.
Then we got stripes and big Xs made on our clothing and got our hair cut.
No.
Yeah.
When I came in the barrack, I met some friends there.
And I said, can I sleep next to you.
And they said, yeah, and you can have my lice, too.
So I look at-- what do you mean?
Well, everyone is having body lice here, because the sanitary conditions are terrible.
Yes-- a lot of noise and a lot of beatings.
The Poles were beating up on the Jews.
In that barracks.
5 o'clock in the morning, we were chased out in a washroom to wash up.
But there were not enough washrooms to wash properly to wash up and to the toilets.
So half could go to the toilets and half to the washroom.
Because there was not enough at that time.
Then you went and had to go for roll call.
You would stand for roll call for a while-- a lot of beatings, too, in the roll call.
The small ones had to be in the front and the bigger ones in the back.
And since I was wearing glasses, they told me, never stay in the front because they'll punch your glasses right in your eyes.
We would get some coffee.
And then we was-- went out to work.
I had-- they were expanding the camp.
I had to work on the-- I don't know what's lorries in English.
But it's we had to load trucks-- trucks and trolleys and push them on the-- in a camp to make the roll call count bigger and put up a fence to make the woman camp bigger.
Yes.
Yeah.
When I worked maybe three or four day in the camp, I got the first surprise.
A few hundred women were chased out the barracks and had to strip in front of us.
And they were standing and delousing.
